burial.plot_notes: Oakwood Cemetery, Syracuse, Onondaga County, New York.

bio.photo_notes: Henry Thomas Hooker (1803-1893), Rochester merchant and Syracuse salt manufacturer

career.notes: One of the early merchants of Rochester NY.
Contributed to Syracuse's salt industry through ingenuity and practical mechanical skill. Syracuse was known as the 'Salt City' of nineteenth-century America.

narrative_blocks.header: Early merchant of Rochester; salt manufacturer in Syracuse
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Thomas Hooker was one of the early merchants of Rochester, New York, before moving to Syracuse where he became a salt manufacturer — contributing, per his obituary, 'not a little in its earlier days' through 'his ingenuity and practical mechanical skill' to a salt industry that made Syracuse the Salt City of nineteenth-century America, one of the nation's most important inland commercial centers. A 'thorough gentleman of the old school,' athlete in his youth, and man of 'strictest integrity, honorable and honored in his dealings,' he was a member of the Presbyterian church whose 'habits of strict temperance' aided 'a vigorous and hearty old age' — so that at ninety, 'his erect form and firm step gave him the appearance of a much younger man.'

narrative_blocks.header: His ingenuity helped build the Syracuse salt industry
narrative_blocks.body: The salt industry that made Syracuse the 'Salt City' of nineteenth-century America benefited specifically from Henry Thomas Hooker's ingenuity and practical mechanical skill — his obituary credits him with contributions to salt manufacturing 'not a little in its earlier days,' when the Onondaga salt springs were being developed into an industrial complex producing much of the nation's table salt and helping finance the Erie Canal at a moment when inland transportation was remaking American commerce. He was also, by all accounts, a man who 'never wearied in his reminiscences of his early sports and adventures' — a former athlete who attributed his longevity to strict temperance habits, the obituary noting that even at the end of his life 'his erect form and firm step gave him the appearance of a much younger man,' as if the athlete were still in there somewhere.

burial.plot_notes: Oakwood Cemetery, Syracuse, Onondaga County, New York.

career.notes: One of the early merchants of Rochester NY.
Contributed to Syracuse's salt industry through ingenuity and practical mechanical skill. Syracuse was known as the 'Salt City' of nineteenth-century America.

narrative_blocks.body: Henry Thomas Hooker was one of the early merchants of Rochester, New York, before moving to Syracuse where he became a salt manufacturer — contributing, per his obituary, 'not a little in its earlier days' through 'his ingenuity and practical mechanical skill' to a salt industry that made Syracuse the Salt City of nineteenth-century America, one of the nation's most important inland commercial centers. A 'thorough gentleman of the old school,' athlete in his youth, and man of 'strictest integrity, honorable and honored in his dealings,' he was a member of the Presbyterian church whose 'habits of strict temperance' aided 'a vigorous and hearty old age' — so that at ninety, 'his erect form and firm step gave him the appearance of a much younger man.'

narrative_blocks.body: The salt industry that made Syracuse the 'Salt City' of nineteenth-century America benefited specifically from Henry Thomas Hooker's ingenuity and practical mechanical skill — his obituary credits him with contributions to salt manufacturing 'not a little in its earlier days,' when the Onondaga salt springs were being developed into an industrial complex producing much of the nation's table salt and helping finance the Erie Canal at a moment when inland transportation was remaking American commerce. He was also, by all accounts, a man who 'never wearied in his reminiscences of his early sports and adventures' — a former athlete who attributed his longevity to strict temperance habits, the obituary noting that even at the end of his life 'his erect form and firm step gave him the appearance of a much younger man,' as if the athlete were still in there somewhere.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived her husband by four years at ninety-four
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Brown Cobb was born in Stonington, Connecticut in March 1803 — the same year as her husband Henry Thomas Hooker — and outlived him by four years, dying on December 29, 1897 at ninety-four; her obituary called her 'a woman of strong but lovely character — a true New England woman,' a charter member of the Fourth Presbyterian Church of Syracuse and later of the Memorial Presbyterian Church where her son Henry Cobb Hooker served as superintendent and elder. Born in the second year of Jefferson's presidency, she died in the year of McKinley's first inauguration, her ninety-four-year life spanning virtually the entire American republic as it grew from a coastal nation to a continental one.

career.notes: Connected with Spencer Clothing Company for approximately 30 years until his death.

career.role: Sunday school superintendent; Elder and Trustee
career.notes: Superintendent from 1887 until year before death; also elder and trustee. Originally superintendent of Scattergood Mission Sunday School until it was organized into Memorial Presbyterian Church in 1887.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Cobb' honors his mother Mary Brown Cobb Hooker (X01623).

narrative_blocks.header: Thirty years with Spencer Clothing; Sunday school superintendent
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Cobb Hooker spent approximately thirty years connected with the Spencer Clothing Company of Syracuse, and simultaneously devoted himself to the Memorial Presbyterian Church — serving as Sunday school superintendent from 1887 until failing health forced him out a year before his death, having shepherded the Sunday school from its origins as the Scattergood Mission through its organization into a full congregation, while also serving as elder and trustee. He died of sarcoma at midnight on May 25, 1908 at his summer cottage at Three Mile Point on Skaneateles Lake — a widower since the death of his wife Frances in 1895, survived only by his brother Thomas — his funeral held from his home at 108 West Castle Street in Syracuse.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: Died at forty-two
narrative_blocks.body: Frances Hopkins Hooker died on June 2, 1895 in Syracuse at age forty-two — 'a member of the May Memorial church and identified with many charitable and religious societies,' per her obituary — leaving her husband Henry Cobb Hooker a widower he would remain for the final thirteen years of his life. They had no children; Frances is survived in the record only by her husband and her sister, Mrs. George Bond of Syracuse, who was present at her death.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

career.organization: Elbridge, New York and Lake Forest, Illinois

career.notes: EH: "since 1878 he has been engaged in manufacturing business at Syracuse, N.Y."

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed at thirty-three; never remarried; lived to 1917
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker married Emma Cornelia 'Nellie' Dennison on September 26, 1872 in Syracuse, and she died less than three years later — on June 20, 1875, at age twenty-seven in East Syracuse, barely twenty-eight months into a marriage that left no children. Thomas lived forty-two more years as a widower, dying in Syracuse on June 21, 1917 at seventy-five — the last of the four children of Henry Thomas and Mary Brown Cobb Hooker to die, outlasting even his brother Henry Cobb by nine years.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

burial.plot_notes: Lake View Cemetery Cleveland Cuyahoga County Ohio per FA — possibly near family from Ohio

narrative_blocks.header: Married 1872; dead at twenty-seven; no children left
narrative_blocks.body: Emma Cornelia 'Nellie' Dennison married Thomas Hooker in Syracuse on September 26, 1872, and died less than three years later on June 20, 1875 in East Syracuse at twenty-seven, leaving no children and a widower who would live forty-two more years without remarrying. She was buried at Lake View Cemetery in Cleveland, Ohio — far from her Syracuse home, possibly near her Ohio-born family — while her husband Thomas lived on in Syracuse until 1917, the last survivor of his generation of Hookers.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

naming_inspiration.description: Named for Rev. Andrew Yates (I00899; Yale 1794), who married his father's cousin Hannah Allin Hooker (H00794). The 'Andrew Yates' name honored this prominent minister in the extended family.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

cross_connections.display_label: His mother — daughter of Governor Joseph Talcott.
His maternal grandfather, Governor of Connecticut 1724-1741.
First cousin once removed via the Talcott family.
Wife of his cousin Wadsworth — also a Hooker descendant.
Father-in-law and partner in the Hooker and Chaffee firm.
his partner in the firm Hooker & Chaffee
brother of his wife Mary Chaffee

narrative_blocks.header: Hooker and Chaffee supplied the Continental Army
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker and Chaffee was one of New England's largest mercantile houses before the Revolution, transacting much business for the Colonial Government. The partners gave freely to the patriot cause and were seriously embarrassed at war's end.

narrative_blocks.header: His firm provisioned alongside Wadsworth's commissary
narrative_blocks.body: The Hooker and Chaffee firm operated alongside Jeremiah Wadsworth's larger commissary operation — both Hooker-Talcott descendants provisioning the same Continental Army. The Connecticut River Valley supply network was effectively a Hooker-tree internal collaboration.

narrative_blocks.header: His Windsor house is on the National Register
narrative_blocks.body: He built the James Hooker House ca. 1772 at 118 Palisado Avenue, Windsor — now part of the Palisado Avenue Historic District on the National Register of Historic Places. The interior was later gutted for Chaffee School classrooms but the Federal-period exterior survives under Greek Revival additions.

institutions.institution_blurb: Silent partner; one of New England's great Revolutionary-era trading houses.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: He built this house c.1772; it survives on Palisado Avenue's National Register district.

bio.title: Captain
bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Captain James Hooker (1742-1805), Windsor CT merchant.

career.notes: Principal of INST070 Hooker and Chaffee. Succeeded to father's Hartford business with brothers; established Windsor branch. See INST070 for full firm history.

parents.mother_research_notes: Lydia (Griswold) Chaffee, b.Dec 17, 1722 Simsbury CT; d.Oct 1, 1801 Windsor CT. Daughter of the Griswold family of Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Brother John was partner in Hooker & Chaffee
narrative_blocks.body: Her older brother John Chaffee was a partner in the firm Hooker & Chaffee — the business linking her birth family directly to her husband's. The 1784 marriage to Captain James Hooker thus joined both personal and commercial lines of the Windsor Connecticut Chaffee household.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived 41 years past husband's 1805 death
narrative_blocks.body: She survived her husband Captain James Hooker by forty-one years — he died December 1805, she lived until October 1846 at eighty-six. After James's death she returned from Rochester New York to Windsor Connecticut and lived with her unmarried sister Hepsibah Chaffee until Hepsibah's 1834 death.

cross_connections.display_label: Both families joined through Talcott siblings.
His wife's brother Matthew also married into the Hooker family line.
Both tied to separate Talcott branches in the same family generation.
Both families joined through Talcott siblings a generation later.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Governor Talcott's daughter Eunice
narrative_blocks.body: Eunice Talcott was the second daughter of Joseph Talcott, Governor of Connecticut from 1725 to 1741. Their marriage directly joined the two founding Hartford families — reinforced when Eunice's brother Matthew simultaneously married Mary Russell, herself a Hooker descendant.

narrative_blocks.header: Named co-heir in the Governor's estate
narrative_blocks.body: On December 25, 1742, Nathaniel and Eunice appeared before the Court of Probate in Hartford to sign Governor Joseph Talcott's estate distribution as co-heirs. It was one of the final official acts binding the Hooker family to the colonial governor's wealth.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale man, militia captain, Assembly representative
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale College in 1729 and held the rank of Captain in the Connecticut Militia as early as 1734. He served as Hartford's representative in the General Assembly across three sessions, while running a substantial Hartford mercantile estate.

narrative_blocks.header: Son James partnered in Hooker & Chaffee
narrative_blocks.body: His son James Hooker was a partner in the mercantile firm Hooker & Chaffee, which grew very successful during the Revolutionary War era. The firm continued the family's Hartford commercial standing into the next generation.

cross_connections.display_label: Sister-in-law — Mary Russell married her brother Matthew Talcott.

narrative_blocks.header: She and brother Matthew both married Hooker descendants
narrative_blocks.body: She married Captain Nathaniel Hooker — a direct Hooker-surname descendant. Her brother Matthew Talcott simultaneously married Mary Russell, herself a Hooker descendant through the Pierpont line. Two of Governor Joseph Talcott's children marrying into the Hooker family in the same generation is why "Talcott" became a permanent name in the Hooker tree.

cross_connections.display_label: Business partner in the Hooker and Chaffee mercantile firm.

narrative_blocks.header: Documented his enslaved Betty Stevenson by name
narrative_blocks.body: His 1791 statement to the Hartford town clerk recorded his ownership of 'a Negro Woman' and her newborn daughter 'called by the Name Betty Stevenson.' He registered the birth as legally required.

narrative_blocks.header: Held a high rank in Connecticut medicine
narrative_blocks.body: He came to Hartford to start a drug store and settled in Windsor in 1755 on the advice of Dr. Isaac Bull. He practiced there for sixty years until his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Born seven weeks after his father's death
narrative_blocks.body: His father Hezekiah Chaffee Sr. died in October 1731 and Hezekiah was born December 6 of the same year in Rehoboth, Massachusetts. He was the youngest of nine children.
